card.title: Why Flash Photos Give You Red Eyes
card.teaser: That creepy glow in old birthday photos is literally a picture of the inside of your eyeball.
card.coverImagePrompt: A vintage 90s birthday party photograph with the subjects' eyes glowing bright red from camera flash, warm nostalgic tones, slight grain

lessons.title: Why does Grandma look possessed?
lessons.body: Every family photo album has them — beloved relatives with glowing red demon eyes staring out from a birthday cake. It's not a haunting, a bad camera, or your lens. That eerie red glow is, quite literally, a photograph of the back of the inside of somebody's eye.

visual.imagePrompt: A vintage flash photo of three people around a birthday cake, all with glowing red pupils, faded color print

lessons.title: The flash is a telescope
lessons.body: When a camera fires a flash at someone's face, the pupil is the only way into a dark, wet chamber called the eyeball. Light races through the pupil, bounces off the highly vascular retina at the back, and exits the pupil straight back toward the camera. That blood-rich reflection is why the returning light glows red.

keyTerms.definition: The light-sensitive layer at the back of the eye, filled with blood vessels
The dark opening in the center of your iris that lets light in

visual.imagePrompt: An anatomical diagram of light entering a pupil, bouncing off a red retina, and exiting back toward a camera

lessons.title: Why only cheap flashes do it
lessons.body: Red eye mostly happens when the flash is near the lens — exactly where compact cameras and phones put it. Professional photographers hold their flash off-camera, so the light enters the pupil at an angle and bounces away from the lens. Modern phones dodge the problem by firing a pre-flash: the pupil closes before the real photo. Less light gets in, less red comes back.

visual.imagePrompt: A diagram comparing on-camera flash (red eye bounces straight back) vs off-camera flash (bounce angled away)

lessons.title: Cats glow for a different reason
lessons.body: If you've ever seen a cat's eyes glow green or yellow in a photo, you've spotted something unusual: a *tapetum lucidum*, a mirror-like layer behind the retina that boosts night vision. Humans don't have one, so we just get the reddish hue of our own blood vessels. Red eye isn't a glitch — it's a tiny anatomy lesson printed in your photo.
lessons.funFact: In some rare cases, a single eye glowing white instead of red in a photo can be an early sign of a serious eye condition called retinoblastoma — which is why pediatricians sometimes ask to see family snapshots.

quiz.question: Why does red-eye appear red?
options: Cameras add red color to bright spots
Light reflects off the blood-rich retina
The iris turns red in bright light
quiz.explanation: The flash passes through the pupil and bounces off the retina's blood-rich surface, tinting the returning light red.

quiz.question: Why is red-eye more common with compact cameras and phones?
options: The flash is very close to the lens
quiz.explanation: When the flash sits right next to the lens, reflected light comes straight back into the camera — causing red-eye.

quiz.question: Why do cats' eyes glow in photos instead of going red?
options: Cats have a reflective layer called the tapetum lucidum
Their blood is a different color
They blink faster than the flash
quiz.explanation: Cats (and many nocturnal animals) have a reflective layer behind the retina that amplifies low light — and makes their eyes glow on camera.

sources.title: Photograph Red Reflex Screening for Retinoblastoma
